Not a nice to have, but a must have if you want to compete in the modern world, which I didn't say that specifically, but we should all keep that in mind, that it should be a compelling software that somebody wants to use to drive profitability and all of the things that are important to a business long term versus just making it easier for people to communicate with one another.

Like what are, which is, it's called basically hub and spoke model in SEO, which is you have like a parent page or like a more foundational piece of content that targets a more broad concept.
And then from there you have like different sub pages that talk about a specific topic within that broader concept.
So when you go.
Go to Assignments, and if you expand, you can see what are the hubs.
So we're, I mean, obviously, as we do more keyword research, there will be more hubs here and, like, more spokes, but this is what we're starting with.
So you can see what is the hub, and then you can expand to see, okay, what is, like, the hub page, and then the spokes.
And I think that can kind of help you because you're likely, like, probably, like, 99% sure that you want to approve something like this.
But for the spokes, it makes it easier for you to see, okay, what are, like, what are they suggesting that's also related to this topic?
And then we have for, and it's basically the same for, like, all clusters.
So we have, like, a hub that's, like, AI for product teams, and then under here we have, like, a hub page that would talk about AI for product managers.
And then we have, like, a couple of spokes that talk about different subtopics.
I think that should make it kind of more organized for you to go through.

So Scrunch, Profound, Meridian, they're all LLM visibility platforms.
That function by sending different prompts to LLMs, then they pull the responses, they analyze them, and they kind of present them in a dashboard like this.
So what we did was we provided Scrunch with a description of your company, a list of your competitors, and then we also provided descriptions of your target audience.
And then Scrunch generated, I think, is it 25?
So,
25 or 35 base prompts, Erik?

I went in last week and added several more.

But we can also add many more prompts in here.

So these prompts are sent to all of these different LLMs like Claude, Google, Gemini, Chagibiti, Perplexity.
And then we kind of get an idea of where your competitive presence stands compared to like your top competitors.
The position breaks down like where you appear in an LLMs response if it's like listing different products.
Or platforms, and sentiment is self-explanatory, citations refer to the number of links to your website that appear in LLM responses.
So at 1%, this is quite low.
However, for most of our clients, they are around 3% to 4%.
On the other hand, your competitors are roughly around 20%.
So the way to increase citations is just to have more content on your website that we can, that LLMs can cite.

They have over 1,000 URLs of content across their blog, glossary, like different subfolders.
And their citation rate is around 15%.
So the more educational content...
The you have on your website, the higher the, you know, chance that you'll be cited by LLMs.

And this is, like, a month-by-month breakdown, but we can also change it to a week-by-week breakdown so you have, like, a better idea how traffic moves week over week.
An engaged session in Google Analytics 4 is a session that lasts longer than 10 seconds, or it includes a page scroll, or it includes
It's the user clicking on a link to go somewhere else on your website or go to a different website.
So that counts as an engaged session.
And typically, this is the average range of engaged sessions that we see across most of our clients, so slightly more than 50%.
If you scroll down, you can see the individual landing page performance, including data around the individual engagement rate for each landing page.
And you can also see a breakdown of sessions by referrers.

traffic that comes to your website from search queries or keywords that don't include your company name.

And we can see like an overall like traffic growth or decrease in URL clicks and impressions, which is what those are the only two metrics that we can track in Google Search Console.
Google Search Console also allows us to track the average position for each page on your website and the average click-through rate.
And you can also see like the individual performance of different landing pages in Google Search Console.
For the conversion events, this is data from your Google Analytics 4 account.
